Model architecture and parameter reference

Source: vignettes/model-architecture.Rmd
model-architecture.Rmd

This vignette is the complete reference for RSurvivEHR. It explains what the model does, how every survivehr_config() parameter affects training, what values the original authors used in their large-scale UK primary-care study (Gadd et al., 2026), and how to choose values for your own data.

Jump to: How the model works · Quick-start configs · Upstream hyperparameters · Full config reference · Architecture · Regularisation · Optimisation · Loss weights · Tokenisation · Time & age · Hardware · Fine-tuning notes

How the model works

SurvivEHR is a GPT-style decoder-only transformer pre-trained on sequences of clinical events (diagnoses, prescriptions, measurements) via causal language modelling. After pre-training, the model encodes the temporal structure of a patient’s medical history into a dense representation. A neural-ODE survival head is then fine-tuned on labelled outcome data (target event and age at event) to produce calibrated time-to-event curves.

EHR sequences --> Tokeniser --> Token + positional embedding (RoPE)
                                              |
                                  n_layer x NeoGPT block
                                  (MHA + SwiGLU MLP + Pre-LN)
                                              |
                                  Last attended hidden state
                                              |
                        +-----------------------------------------+
                        |  Competing-risk ODE head (one ODE/event)|
                        |  or                                      |
                        |  Single-risk ODE head (one ODE)         |
                        +-----------------------------------------+
                                              |
                                  CIF curves per time horizon

Transformer block (NeoGPT)

RSurvivEHR always uses the NeoGPT block, which differs from the original nanoGPT block in three ways:

  • Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) — encodes relative token positions inside attention, without a separate positional embedding layer.
  • SwiGLU activation — replaces GELU in the MLP for improved gradient flow.
  • Pre-LN — LayerNorm is applied before each sub-layer (attention and MLP) rather than after, which stabilises training.

Neural-ODE survival head

The survival head uses the DeSurv neural ODE framework. For the competing-risk setting, one ODE is instantiated per outcome event, each with an encoder MLP, a lightweight ODE right-hand-side network, and a sigmoid cumulative incidence function (CIF) output. For single-risk, a single ODE produces one survival curve.

The ODE integrates over a normalised time grid [0,1][0, 1], which maps back to [0,𝚝𝚒𝚖𝚎_𝚜𝚌𝚊𝚕𝚎][0, \texttt{time\_scale}] in your original age units (see time_scale).

Vocabulary construction

The vocabulary is built automatically during survivehr_pretrain() and stored inside every model bundle:

  1. All unique event codes are collected from the events data frame.
  2. Special tokens are prepended at fixed indices:
    • <PAD> → index 0 (always present; padding_idx = 0)
    • <UNK> → index 1 (when include_unk = TRUE)
    • <CLS> → index 2 (when include_cls_sep = TRUE)
    • <SEP> → index 3 (when include_cls_sep = TRUE)
  3. Clinical event tokens are assigned indices ≥ 2 (or ≥ 4 with CLS/SEP), ordered by frequency — most common event first (lowest index).

At fine-tuning, outcome codes absent from the pre-training vocabulary are automatically appended and the embedding layer is extended with a weight-safe copy from the pre-trained backbone.

Quick-start configurations

Copy the block that best matches your setup. All parameters not shown use their defaults (see Full config reference).

Laptop / small dataset (< 5 000 patients) 

library(RSurvivEHR)

cfg_small <- survivehr_config(
  block_size    = 64,
  n_layer       = 2,
  n_head        = 2,
  n_embd        = 64,
  dropout       = 0.1,    # regularise on small data
  learning_rate = 3e-4,
  epochs        = 20,
  batch_size    = 8,
  surv_layer    = "competing-risk",
  time_scale    = 1.0     # backbone normalisation (set outcome_horizon at fine-tune time)
)

Workstation / medium dataset (5 000 – 50 000 patients) 

cfg_medium <- survivehr_config(
  block_size    = 128,
  n_layer       = 4,
  n_head        = 4,
  n_embd        = 256,
  dropout       = 0.0,
  learning_rate = 3e-4,
  epochs        = 10,
  batch_size    = 32,
  surv_layer    = "competing-risk",
  time_scale    = 1.0     # backbone normalisation (set outcome_horizon at fine-tune time)
)

HPC / large dataset — upstream CPRD settings (> 100 000 patients) 

# Pre-training
cfg_pretrain <- survivehr_config(
  block_size    = 256,
  n_layer       = 6,
  n_head        = 6,
  n_embd        = 384,
  dropout       = 0.0,
  learning_rate = 3e-4,
  epochs        = 10,
  batch_size    = 64,
  surv_layer    = "competing-risk",
  time_scale    = 5.0
)

# Fine-tuning (larger context window, increased lr and batch size)
cfg_finetune <- survivehr_config(
  block_size    = 512,
  n_layer       = 6,
  n_head        = 6,
  n_embd        = 384,
  dropout       = 0.0,
  learning_rate = 5e-4,
  epochs        = 20,
  batch_size    = 512,
  surv_layer    = "competing-risk",
  outcome_horizon = 5.0  # 5-year prediction window; time_scale inherited from pretrained bundle
)

Upstream hyperparameters

The table below shows the hyperparameters used in the original CPRD study (Gadd et al., 2026) alongside the corresponding survivehr_config() parameter. Upstream defaults are also available in the published configuration files config_CompetingRisk11M.yaml and config_SingleRisk.yaml.

Component Hyperparameter survivehr_config() param Pre-training Fine-tuning
Architecture Layers n_layer 6 6
Attention heads n_head 6 6
Hidden size n_embd 384 384
Max sequence length block_size 256 512
Regularisation Dropout dropout 0.0 0.0
Optimisation Batch size batch_size 64 512
Epochs epochs 10 20
Optimiser AdamW (fixed) AdamW AdamW
Learning rate learning_rate 3 × 10^−4 5 × 10^−4
Loss weights Backbone survival head surv_layer "competing-risk" "competing-risk"
Survival loss weight surv_weight 1.0 1.0
Value regression weight value_weight 0.1 —
Tokenisation Reserve <UNK> token include_unk TRUE (inherited)
Add <CLS>/<SEP> tokens include_cls_sep FALSE (inherited)
Time / age Age normalisation divisor time_scale 1.0 (inherited)
ODE prediction window outcome_horizon — 5.0
Hardware Compute device device "auto" (GPU) "auto" (GPU)

Note on the optimiser: the upstream CPRD study used separate backbone and head learning rates, LR schedulers, warmup steps, and early stopping. The RSurvivEHR backend uses a single AdamW optimiser over all parameters with a constant learning rate — no scheduler or early stopping is implemented. The learning_rate you set applies uniformly to the whole model.

survivehr_config() — full parameter reference

The annotated call below shows every parameter with its RSurvivEHR default and a note on the upstream value.

library(RSurvivEHR)

cfg <- survivehr_config(
  # -- Transformer architecture -------------------------------------------
  block_size    = 128,       # context window (tokens); upstream pre-train: 256, fine-tune: 512
  n_layer       = 4,         # transformer blocks;      upstream: 6
  n_head        = 4,         # attention heads;         upstream: 6
  n_embd        = 256,       # embedding dimension;     upstream: 384

  # -- Regularisation ------------------------------------------------------
  dropout       = 0.0,       # applied to attention, MLP residuals; upstream: 0.0

  # -- Optimisation --------------------------------------------------------
  learning_rate = 3e-4,      # single AdamW LR for all parameters
  epochs        = 1,         # upstream pre-train: 10;  fine-tune: 20
  batch_size    = 16,        # upstream pre-train: 64;  fine-tune: 512

  # -- Loss weights --------------------------------------------------------
  surv_layer    = "competing-risk",  # backbone survival head; or "single-risk"
  surv_weight   = 1.0,       # weight on the survival loss term
  value_weight  = 0.0,       # weight on value regression loss (upstream pre-train: 0.1)

  # -- Tokenisation --------------------------------------------------------
  include_unk     = TRUE,    # reserve <UNK> for unseen events; upstream: TRUE
  include_cls_sep = FALSE,   # add <CLS>/<SEP> tokens; upstream: FALSE (GPT-style)

  # -- Age / time handling -------------------------------------------------
  time_scale      = 1.0,       # backbone age normalisation divisor (raw_age / time_scale)
  outcome_horizon = NULL,      # ODE prediction window (fine-tune only); NULL = inherit time_scale

  # -- Hardware ------------------------------------------------------------
  device        = "auto"     # "auto", "cpu", or "cuda"
)

Parameter details

block_size – context window

RSurvivEHR default Upstream pre-training Upstream fine-tuning Notes
128 256 512 Fine-tuning uses a longer window to include more history.

The context window is the maximum number of tokens (clinical events) seen by the model at once. Sequences longer than block_size are right-truncated – only the most recent events are retained. Sequences shorter than block_size are left-padded with <PAD> tokens (index 0).

Tip: set block_size to the 95th percentile of sequence lengths in your dataset.

n_layer – number of transformer blocks

RSurvivEHR default Upstream (both stages)
4 6

Each block consists of multi-head self-attention (MHA), a two-layer MLP, LayerNorm (Pre-LN), and residual connections. More layers gives greater representational capacity but requires more data and compute.

n_head – number of attention heads

RSurvivEHR default Upstream (both stages) Notes
4 6 Must divide n_embd evenly (384 / 6 = 64 per head).

n_embd – embedding / hidden dimension

RSurvivEHR default Upstream (both stages) Notes
256 384 Total params ~= 12 x n_layer x n_embd^2.

Controls the width of every layer. The MLP hidden dimension inside each block is 4 x n_embd. Common sizes: 64 (debug), 128 (tiny), 256 (small), 384 (medium, upstream), 512-768 (large).

dropout

RSurvivEHR default Upstream
0.0 0.0

Not needed when training on millions of patients. Set 0.1-0.3 for small datasets (< 10 000 patients).

Optimisation: learning_rate, epochs, batch_size

learning_rate

RSurvivEHR default Upstream pre-training Upstream fine-tuning
3e-4 3 × 10^−4 5 × 10^−4

The optimiser is AdamW. The learning_rate you supply is applied uniformly to all model parameters via a single optimiser — there is no separate backbone rate or learning rate schedule in the RSurvivEHR backend. The upstream study used a lower backbone rate (5 × 10^−5) during fine-tuning to preserve pre-trained weights; this is not currently implemented here.

epochs

RSurvivEHR default Upstream pre-training Upstream fine-tuning
1 10 20

Number of full passes over the training data. Early stopping is not implemented — set epochs explicitly. Scale up proportionally for smaller datasets where each epoch sees fewer gradient steps.

batch_size

RSurvivEHR default Upstream pre-training Upstream fine-tuning
16 64 512

The upstream fine-tuning batch of 512 requires multi-GPU hardware. On a single laptop GPU (8 GB VRAM), batch_size = 16–32 with block_size = 128 is a safe starting point.

Loss weights: surv_layer, surv_weight, value_weight

surv_layer — backbone time-to-next-event head

SurvivEHR pre-training goes beyond standard causal language modelling (predicting which event is next). The backbone also trains a survival head to predict when the next event occurs, using age as the time axis. surv_layer controls how that timing problem is framed:

Value Description
"competing-risk" (default) Treats each sequence position as a genuine competing-risk problem: only one event can be next, so all other vocabulary events implicitly compete. One neural ODE is instantiated per vocabulary token.
"single-risk" Fits one independent ODE per vocabulary token in a 1-vs-all fashion, treating all other events as censored rather than competing.

Important: surv_layer controls the backbone head used in both pre-training and fine-tuning — the backbone architecture must remain consistent across both stages. The outcome-level head that produces patient predictions is controlled separately by the risk_model argument to survivehr_finetune(). Because they are entirely separate nn.Module objects, surv_layer and risk_model can differ — e.g. pre-train with surv_layer = "competing-risk" and fine-tune with risk_model = "single-risk" without conflict.

The upstream config uses the abbreviated codes "cr" and "sr" — both are also accepted by RSurvivEHR.

surv_weight and value_weight

The total pre-training loss is a weighted sum of three terms:

Loss term Weight parameter Default Description
Causal LM — (fixed at 1) 1.0 Predict the identity of the next event token.
Backbone survival surv_weight 1.0 Predict when the next event occurs (neural ODE).
Value regression value_weight 0.0 Predict the numeric value of measurement events (e.g. BMI).

The upstream CPRD study used surv_weight = 1.0 and value_weight = 0.1 for models that also learned measurement magnitudes. Set value_weight > 0 only if your events data frame includes meaningful numeric value entries.

Tokenisation: include_unk, include_cls_sep

Pre-training only. Both settings are fixed when the vocabulary is built during survivehr_pretrain() and stored in the model bundle. Values in a fine-tune config are ignored.

include_unk

RSurvivEHR default Upstream
TRUE TRUE

Reserves <UNK> at index 1. Any event code seen at fine-tune or prediction time that was absent from the pre-training vocabulary is silently mapped to <UNK>. Always leave TRUE for production use.

include_cls_sep

RSurvivEHR default Upstream
FALSE FALSE

Prepends <CLS> and appends <SEP> around each patient sequence, following the BERT / BEHRT convention. Not used in the upstream GPT-style pre-training. Set TRUE only if you are adapting RSurvivEHR for BERT-style models.

Time & age: time_scale and outcome_horizon

These two parameters were previously a single value (time_scale) that controlled both the backbone input scale and the prediction window. They are now independent.

time_scale — backbone age normalisation

All raw event ages are divided by time_scale before entering the transformer:

age_norm=raw_agetime_scale\text{age\_norm} = \frac{\text{raw\_age}}{\text{time\_scale}}

This controls only how the backbone sees age values — it has nothing to do with the prediction window. Must be identical between pre-training and fine-tuning. The value is stored in the pretrained bundle and inherited automatically — you do not need to set it in a fine-tune config.

Age unit Recommended time_scale
Years (e.g. 50.0) 1.0 — backbone sees year values directly
Days since birth (e.g. 18262) 365.25 — backbone sees normalised fractions

outcome_horizon — ODE prediction window (fine-tune only)

The survival ODE integrates over a normalised grid [0,1][0, 1], where 1.01.0 maps back to outcome_horizon in your raw age units. This is what determines what cdf_last means and what values are valid in eval_times.

outcome_horizon can differ freely from time_scale. The canonical use case is:

# Pre-train: year-by-year backbone normalisation
cfg_pt <- survivehr_config(time_scale = 1.0)

# Fine-tune: 5-year risk window, independent of time_scale
cfg_ft <- survivehr_config(outcome_horizon = 5.0)
outcome_horizon cdf_last meaning Valid eval_times
1.0 1-year risk (0, 1] years
5.0 5-year risk (0, 5] years
1826.25 5-year risk (ages in days) (0, 1826.25] days

If outcome_horizon = NULL (default), it inherits time_scale for backward compatibility. outcome_horizon is stored in every fine-tune bundle and used automatically at prediction time — no need to specify it when calling survivehr_predict().

Hardware: device

Value Behaviour
"auto" (default) Uses GPU (CUDA) if available, otherwise falls back to CPU.
"cpu" Forces CPU computation.
"cuda" Forces GPU — raises an error if no CUDA device is available.

Fine-tuning notes

The same survivehr_config() object is passed to both survivehr_pretrain() and survivehr_finetune(). A few parameters behave differently at fine-tune time:

Parameter Fine-tuning behaviour
time_scale Inherited automatically from the pretrained bundle; value in fine-tune config is ignored.
outcome_horizon Fine-tune only. Sets the ODE prediction window length independently of time_scale. Stored in the fine-tune bundle; read automatically by survivehr_predict(). Defaults to time_scale if not set.
include_unk Inherited from the pretrained bundle vocabulary.
include_cls_sep Inherited from the pretrained bundle vocabulary.
block_size Can be increased at fine-tune time (e.g. 256 → 512) to provide a longer context.
learning_rate Applied uniformly to all parameters. No separate backbone rate or scheduler is implemented.
epochs Early stopping is not implemented; set this explicitly.

The following Python-side options exist in the upstream backend but are not yet exposed through survivehr_config() in RSurvivEHR:

Option Upstream default Description
PEFT / adapter Full fine-tune "fix" freezes the backbone; "adapter" adds LoRA-style adapter layers.
Layer-wise LR decay Disabled Applies exponentially smaller learning rates toward the input layers.

References

Gadd, C., Gokhale, K., Acharya, A. et al. (2026). SurvivEHR: a competing risks, time-to-event foundation model for multiple long-term conditions from primary care electronic health records. npj Digital Medicine. doi:10.1038/s41746-026-02709-z

On this page
